title: Listening - Part 3: The End of the Night
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a biologist, Abbi, and an urban planner, Elliot, about the environmental impact of artificial light. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview exploring the disruption of migration patterns, the decline of insect populations, and the 'dark sky' movement.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: For most of human history, the night was truly dark. Today, eighty percent of the world lives under 'skyglow.' Abbi, as a biologist, why does this matter for wildlife?

Abbi: [15] Evolution has occurred over billions of years with a reliable cycle of light and dark. When we break that cycle, we break nature. [/15] For example, sea turtles hatch on beaches and rely on the light of the horizon to find the ocean. Artificial lights from hotels lead them inland toward roads instead. It’s a death sentence. It’s not just turtles; billions of birds migrate at night using the stars, and city lights pull them off course into buildings.

Interviewer: Elliot, you design city lighting. Isn't more light always better for safety?

Elliot: [16] That’s a common misconception. Excessive glare actually creates 'deep shadows' where it’s harder to see. Over-lighting doesn't make us safer; it just wastes energy. [/16] We are moving toward 'shielded' lighting—lamps that point only where the light is needed, on the ground, rather than spilling into the sky or people's bedroom windows. 

Interviewer: Abbi, we’re hearing about an 'insect apocalypse.' Is light a factor?

Abbi: [17] A huge one. A single streetlamp can act as an 'ecological trap.' Insects are drawn to the light, where they are either eaten by predators or die of exhaustion. [/17] This disrupts the entire food chain. If the insects die, the birds that eat them suffer, and the plants they pollinate stop reproducing. We are essentially 'vacuuming' insects out of the surrounding countryside with our city lights.

Interviewer: Elliot, what is the 'Dark Sky' movement?

Elliot: [18] It’s a group of cities and parks dedicated to preserving the natural night. They use bulbs with 'warmer' colors—reds and oranges—because blue-rich white light is the most disruptive to biological clocks. [/18] Some towns in Europe are now turning off streetlights after midnight in residential areas. It saves money and allows people to actually see the Milky Way again.

Interviewer: Abbi, does this affect human health too?

Abbi: [19] Absolutely. We aren't separate from nature. Artificial light at night suppresses melatonin, which increases the risk of sleep disorders, obesity, and even certain types of cancer. [/19] We need the dark to repair our DNA. Our bodies are essentially being told it’s daytime twenty-four hours a day.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Elliot? Is the future of our cities going to be darker?

Elliot: [20] Not darker, but 'smarter.' We’ll have motion-sensing lights that only brighten when a person or car is present. We can have safety *and* a healthy ecosystem if we stop treating light as a free resource with no consequences. [/20]

Interviewer: Abbi, Elliot, thank you.

questions.question: Abbi says that sea turtles are endangered by artificial light because they
options: A) are unable to see their predators in the dark.
B) move toward hotels instead of toward the sea.
C) cannot find enough food on brightly lit beaches.
D) are frightened by the noise of city traffic.
questions.explanation: She states that hotel lights lead them inland toward roads instead of the horizon/ocean.

questions.question: What is Elliot's opinion on high-intensity street lighting?
options: A) It is the most effective way to prevent crime.
B) It creates shadows that can actually make areas less safe.
C) It is necessary for drivers to see clearly at night.
D) It is cheaper than using shielded lighting.
questions.explanation: He calls it a 'misconception' and says glare creates 'deep shadows' that make it harder to see.

questions.question: Abbi uses the term 'ecological trap' to describe how
options: A) predators use the dark to catch their prey.
B) streetlamps cause insects to die of tiredness.
C) birds are forced to migrate during the day.
D) plants grow too quickly under artificial light.
questions.explanation: She explains that insects are drawn to light and die from exhaustion or predation.

questions.question: The 'Dark Sky' movement prefers 'warmer' bulb colors because they
options: A) are much brighter than blue-rich white lights.
B) have a less negative impact on biological rhythms.
C) are more modern and visually attractive.
D) use less electricity than traditional bulbs.
questions.explanation: Elliot states that blue-rich light is 'the most disruptive to biological clocks'.

questions.question: According to Abbi, how does artificial light affect the human body?
options: A) It prevents the body from repairing DNA during the night.
B) It makes people more likely to lose weight too quickly.
C) It improves our ability to focus during the day.
D) It helps to prevent the development of cancer.
questions.explanation: She mentions it suppresses melatonin and that 'we need the dark to repair our DNA'.

questions.question: Elliot predicts that future cities will use lighting that
options: A) is turned off completely in all areas after dark.
B) is only activated when it is actually needed.
C) is twice as bright as current street lighting.
D) is provided by the government at no cost.
questions.explanation: He describes 'smarter' motion-sensing lights that only brighten when a person or car is present.
